questions.prompt: What does a linear search return in our Python implementation when the target is not present in the list?
questions.answer_explanation: The implementation returns None as a sentinel value to safely signal that the element was not found.

questions.prompt: Which built-in Python function returns a brand-new ordered list without modifying the original collection?
questions.answer_explanation: The sorted() function creates and returns a new list, leaving the original list unchanged.

questions.prompt: Which Python list method rearranges a list in-place and returns None?
questions.answer_explanation: The .sort() method modifies the list it is called on and does not return a new list.

questions.prompt: What is the worst-case number of comparisons for a linear search on a list of size n?
questions.answer_explanation: In the worst case (when the item is at the end or missing), linear search must inspect every element.

questions.prompt: What is the worst-case complexity shape for a binary search on a sorted list of size n?
questions.answer_explanation: Binary search halves the search space at each step, yielding logarithmic growth.

questions.prompt: Why must a list be sorted before performing a binary search?
questions.answer_explanation: Binary search relies on order to determine whether to discard the lower or upper half of the remaining elements.

choices.text: to rule out half the items

questions.prompt: What is the correct Python syntax to swap two variables without using a third temporary variable?
questions.answer_explanation: Python allows multiple assignments in a single line, like x, y = y, x, which evaluates the right-hand side first and then assigns.

questions.prompt: Which Big-O complexity represents a constant running time that does not grow with the input size?
questions.answer_explanation: O(1) represents constant time, meaning the number of steps is independent of the input size.

questions.prompt: Which Big-O complexity represents quadratic growth, where work grows with the square of the input size?
questions.answer_explanation: O(n²) represents quadratic growth, which is characteristic of selection sort.

questions.prompt: Which sorting algorithm repeatedly finds the smallest remaining item and swaps it into its correct position?
questions.answer_explanation: Selection sort works by selecting the minimum value in the unsorted section and placing it at the front.

questions.prompt: What divide-and-conquer strategy does merge sort use to sort a list?
questions.answer_explanation: Merge sort divides the unsorted list into single-element lists, and recursively merges them into sorted lists.

questions.prompt: Which argument is used in Python's sorted() to specify a custom sorting criteria?
questions.answer_explanation: The key argument accepts a function that computes a sort key for each item.

questions.prompt: What expression correctly extracts a custom attribute from a card object for sorting?
questions.answer_explanation: A lambda function like `lambda card: card.rank` takes a card object and retrieves its rank attribute.

questions.prompt: In selection sort, if you run it on the list `[5, 3, 8, 1, 9]`, what is the list after the first pass?
questions.answer_explanation: In the first pass, selection sort finds the smallest element (1) and swaps it with the element at index 0 (5), resulting in `[1, 3, 8, 5, 9]`.

questions.prompt: What is the worst-case time complexity of selection sort?
questions.answer_explanation: Selection sort scans the remaining unsorted list for each index, resulting in O(n²) operations.

questions.prompt: What is the time complexity of merge sort?
questions.answer_explanation: Merge sort runs in O(n log n) because there are log n levels of recursion, each requiring O(n) merging work.

questions.prompt: Which search algorithm is the only option when the list is completely unsorted?
questions.answer_explanation: If elements have no ordering, we cannot skip checking any of them; we must search linearly.

questions.prompt: What is the average number of comparisons in a linear search on a list of size n if the item is present?
questions.answer_explanation: On average, a successful linear search will find the item in the middle of the list, requiring n / 2 comparisons.

questions.prompt: Which mathematical concept is used to describe the shape of growth in algorithmic work?
questions.answer_explanation: Big-O notation describes how the resource requirements (like time or memory) scale with input size.

questions.prompt: What is the main advantage of binary search over linear search for large datasets?
questions.answer_explanation: For large list sizes, binary search scales logarithmically, which is dramatically faster than linear growth.

questions.prompt: What does the Church-Turing thesis state about models of computation?
questions.answer_explanation: The Church-Turing thesis asserts that any device capable of executing algorithms shares the exact same theoretical limits.

choices.text: any computer shares the same limits
python runs faster than machine code
all algorithms run in polynomial time

questions.prompt: Which complexity class represents problems that can be solved in a reasonable (polynomial) number of steps?
questions.answer_explanation: The class P consists of decision problems solvable by a deterministic Turing machine in polynomial time.

questions.prompt: Which algorithms modify the list in-place during execution?
questions.answer_explanation: Selection sort and the list .sort() method perform sorting in-place, whereas sorted() returns a new list.

questions.prompt: Which statements describe characteristics of binary search?
questions.answer_explanation: Binary search runs in O(log n) time and strictly requires sorted input.

questions.prompt: Which parameters can be passed to Python's built-in sorted() function?
questions.answer_explanation: sorted() accepts key (to specify a function to extract a comparison key) and reverse (to change sort order).

questions.prompt: Which running times grow slower than quadratic growth as input size increases?
questions.answer_explanation: O(log n), O(n), and O(n log n) all grow slower than quadratic O(n²).

questions.prompt: Which of the following are valid sentinel values or search results in our linear and binary search code?
questions.answer_explanation: We return the integer index if found, and None as a sentinel value if not found.

questions.prompt: What are the key steps in the divide-and-conquer strategy of merge sort?
questions.answer_explanation: Merge sort splits the list recursively until single elements are reached, then merges sorted sublists.

questions.prompt: Which problems belong to the complexity class NP (can be verified in polynomial time)?
questions.answer_explanation: Scheduling classes, Sudoku, and jigsaw puzzles are in NP (easy to check, hard to solve), whereas binary search is in P.

questions.prompt: Which are features of a Turing machine?
questions.answer_explanation: A Turing machine consists of a tape, a read-write head, and a state-transition rulebook. It does not have registers, cache, or CPU.

questions.prompt: What was the core ambition of David Hilbert's mathematical program?
questions.answer_explanation: Hilbert wanted to build a complete, consistent, and decidable formal system for all mathematics.

questions.prompt: What did Kurt Gödel prove about rich consistent formal systems in 1931?
questions.answer_explanation: Gödel's incompleteness theorems proved that any consistent system rich enough for arithmetic will have true statements that are unprovable within the system.

questions.prompt: Which famous decision problem asked if there is an algorithm to decide if any mathematical statement is provable?
questions.answer_explanation: The Entscheidungsproblem was the decision problem posed by David Hilbert.

questions.prompt: What did Alan Turing use to prove that the Entscheidungsproblem has no solution?
questions.answer_explanation: Turing proved that the halting problem is undecidable, which in turn meant the Entscheidungsproblem was unsolvable.

questions.prompt: What is the term for a problem where no algorithm can ever give a correct answer in every case?
questions.answer_explanation: An undecidable problem is one for which no general algorithm can always give a correct yes/no answer.

questions.prompt: What did Alan Turing's 'universal machine' blueprint directly inspire?
questions.answer_explanation: The universal machine, which reads program code as data to mimic other machines, is the conceptual ancestor of general-purpose computer CPUs.

questions.prompt: Which philosophical paradox is the basis for the proofs of both Gödel and Turing?
questions.answer_explanation: The liar paradox ('this statement is false') inspired both Gödel's unprovable sentence and Turing's contrary program.

questions.prompt: What did Fred Cohen prove about computer virus detection in 1987?
questions.answer_explanation: Using a diagonal proof similar to Turing's, Cohen proved that a perfect virus detector (never wrong, never false alarm) cannot exist.

questions.prompt: What demands did David Hilbert make of his proposed mathematical system?
questions.answer_explanation: Hilbert demanded the system be complete (prove all truths), consistent (no contradictions), and decidable (step-by-step decision algorithm).

questions.prompt: What does the controversy between John Lucas and Hilary Putnam concern?
questions.answer_explanation: Lucas argued that Gödel's theorem shows human minds are not machines, whereas Putnam and others replied that we cannot prove human consistency.
